question: [File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker who is already an authorized operator of victim Alice sign and submit an Authorization struct (authorizer=Alice, authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=true, nonce=currentNonce[Alice], deadline=far_future) to EcrecoverAuthorizer, passing the check `signer == authorization.authorizer || IMidnight(MIDNIGHT).isAuthorized(authorization.authorizer, signer)`, thereby granting themselves permanent self-authorization and bypassing any future gate restriction on Alice's account? Preconditions: Alice has previously authorized attacker via Midnight.setIsAuthorized; market has IEnterGate that blocks attacker but not Alice. Call sequence: (1) Alice authorizes attacker directly on Midnight; (2) attacker signs Authorization{authorizer=Alice, authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=true, nonce=N, deadline=T}; (3) attacker submits to EcrecoverAuthorizer; (4) attacker calls take() with taker=Alice, gate checks canIncreaseDebt(Alice). Invariant tested: gate restrictions must not be circumventable via authorized-operator signature submission. Scoped impact: gate bypass — attacker enters gated market on behalf of Alice, increasing Alice's debt without Alice's per-action consent. Proof idea: fuzz Authorization.nonce and deadline; assert that an authorized operator cannot use EcrecoverAuthorizer to re-authorize themselves or escalate privileges beyond what Alice explicitly intended.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized operator Bob, who was granted authorization by Alice via Midnight.setIsAuthorized, call Midnight.setIsAuthorized(maliciousRatifier, true, Alice) directly (no signature required) to authorize a malicious ratifier contract on Alice's behalf, then construct an Offer with offer.maker=Alice and offer.ratifier=maliciousRatifier, passing the `isAuthorized[offer.maker][offer.ratifier]` check in take(), and have the malicious ratifier approve any arbitrary offer terms? Preconditions: Alice authorized Bob; market has IEnterGate; maliciousRatifier.isRatified always returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS. Call sequence: (1) Alice authorizes Bob; (2) Bob calls Midnight.setIsAuthorized(maliciousRatifier, true, Alice); (3) Bob constructs Offer{maker=Alice, ratifier=maliciousRatifier, buy=false, maxAssets=large}; (4) Bob calls take() filling the offer. Invariant tested: ratifier authorization must only be grantable by the maker with explicit per-ratifier consent; coarse authorization must not allow operator to install arbitrary ratifiers. Scoped impact: gate bypass and unauthorized offer fill — attacker fills offers under Alice's maker identity with attacker-controlled terms. Proof idea: unit test asserting that an authorized operator cannot install a new ratifier for the authorizer without a separate explicit consent mechanism.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker front-run a victim's pending EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized transaction by submitting a transaction that increments the victim's nonce (using the attacker's own already-authorized status to sign a valid Authorization for the victim with the same nonce), causing the victim's transaction to revert with InvalidNonce(), permanently invalidating the victim's signed authorization message and forcing the victim to re-sign? Preconditions: attacker is already authorized by victim (isAuthorized[victim][attacker]=true); victim has a pending setIsAuthorized tx with nonce=N in the mempool. Call sequence: (1) attacker observes victim's pending tx with nonce N; (2) attacker signs Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=X, isAuthorized=true, nonce=N, deadline=T} and submits with higher gas; (3) victim's tx reverts with InvalidNonce(); (4) victim's signed message is now permanently invalid. Invariant tested: nonce management must not allow an authorized operator to grief the authorizer's pending authorization transactions. Scoped impact: fund freeze / DoS — victim cannot establish or revoke authorizations via signed messages, potentially locking them out of time-sensitive gate-restricted operations. Proof idea: fuzz front-run timing; assert that an authorized operator cannot consume the authorizer's nonce to invalidate pending authorization messages.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized operator Bob systematically increment Alice's nonce in EcrecoverAuthorizer by repeatedly submitting valid Authorization structs (signed by Bob as authorized signer for Alice) with sequential nonces, causing Alice's nonce to advance far ahead of any pre-signed authorization messages Alice has distributed, rendering all of Alice's pre-signed authorizations permanently invalid? Preconditions: Bob is authorized by Alice on Midnight; Alice has distributed pre-signed authorization messages with nonces N, N+1, N+2. Call sequence: (1) Bob signs and submits Authorization{authorizer=Alice, nonce=N, ...}; (2) Bob repeats for N+1, N+2, ...; (3) Alice's pre-signed messages all fail with InvalidNonce(). Invariant tested: an authorized operator must not be able to exhaust or skip the authorizer's nonce sequence to invalidate pre-signed messages. Scoped impact: gate bypass enablement / DoS — Alice's ability to manage delegated authorization via pre-signed messages is destroyed; attacker can then authorize themselves. Proof idea: stateful fuzz test incrementing nonce via authorized-operator submissions; assert pre-signed messages remain valid.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Does the EIP-712 domain separator in EcrecoverAuthorizer use `block.chainid` (live chain ID) while Midnight.toId uses `INITIAL_CHAIN_ID` (captured at construction), creating a post-hard-fork state where existing Midnight markets remain valid (same IDs) but all previously signed EcrecoverAuthorizer authorization messages become invalid on the new chain, while the old chain retains valid signatures — and can an attacker on the original chain replay a victim's authorization signature that was signed for the new chain (if the victim re-signs with the new chainId but the domain separator happens to collide)? Preconditions: hard fork occurs changing block.chainid; victim re-signs authorization for new chain; attacker operates on original chain. Call sequence: (1) hard fork changes chainId from C to C'; (2) victim signs Authorization with new domain separator (C', EcrecoverAuthorizer); (3) attacker checks if C' domain separator matches any prior domain separator on original chain. Invariant tested: EIP-712 domain separation must prevent cross-chain replay after hard fork; INITIAL_CHAIN_ID vs block.chainid inconsistency must not create exploitable state. Scoped impact: unauthorized authorization — attacker replays victim's signature on wrong chain to grant unauthorized access. Proof idea: differential test comparing domain separator values across chainId values; assert no collision between pre-fork and post-fork domain separators.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker exploit the fact that the EIP-712 domain in EcrecoverAuthorizer omits `name` and `version` fields (using only `EIP712Domain(uint256 chainId,address verifyingContract)`) to construct a collision with another protocol's EIP-712 domain that uses the same minimal domain typehash, same chainId, and happens to deploy at the same address (e.g., via CREATE2 with attacker-controlled salt), allowing cross-protocol signature replay of an Authorization struct? Preconditions: attacker controls a CREATE2 factory; another protocol uses the same minimal EIP712Domain typehash and identical struct layout; victim has signed an authorization for the other protocol. Call sequence: (1) attacker deploys a contract at the same address as EcrecoverAuthorizer on a different network or via CREATE2; (2) victim signs an authorization for the other protocol; (3) attacker replays the signature on EcrecoverAuthorizer. Invariant tested: EIP-712 domain must be sufficiently unique to prevent cross-protocol replay; omitting name/version increases collision surface. Scoped impact: unauthorized authorization — victim's signature for another protocol grants attacker authorization on Midnight. Proof idea: construct two EIP-712 domains with identical typehash, chainId, and verifyingContract; assert digest collision is impossible or document the risk.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit an Authorization with `deadline == block.timestamp` (exact equality), which passes the check `block.timestamp <= authorization.deadline`, and exploit the fact that on PoS Ethereum the block proposer can set `block.timestamp` to any value >= parent.timestamp + 1 and <= current_unix_time + some tolerance, allowing a validator-attacker to include a transaction with a deadline that has already passed from the perspective of the next block, effectively extending the validity window of a signature beyond the signer's intent? Preconditions: attacker is a block proposer or has MEV relationship with one; victim signed Authorization with deadline=T; attacker wants to submit after T. Call sequence: (1) victim signs Authorization with deadline=T; (2) at time T+1, attacker bribes proposer to set block.timestamp=T; (3) attacker submits Authorization; (4) check passes since T <= T. Invariant tested: deadline must represent a hard expiry that cannot be extended by block timestamp manipulation. Scoped impact: unauthorized authorization — expired signature is accepted, granting attacker authorization past the intended expiry. Proof idea: fuzz block.timestamp around deadline boundary; assert that deadline=block.timestamp is accepted and test validator timestamp manipulation scenarios.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized operator Bob, authorized by Alice, call Midnight.setIsAuthorized(bob, false, alice) to revoke his own authorization, then immediately call EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized with a pre-signed Authorization{authorizer=Alice, authorized=Bob, isAuthorized=true, nonce=N} that Bob signed before revoking himself, re-granting himself authorization in the same transaction via multicall, creating a self-authorization loop that bypasses any gate-level access control that was supposed to prevent Bob from acting? Preconditions: Bob is authorized by Alice; Bob has a pre-signed authorization message; market has IEnterGate blocking Bob. Call sequence: (1) Bob calls multicall([setIsAuthorized(bob,false,alice), EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(pre-signed-auth)]); (2) Bob is re-authorized; (3) Bob calls take() with taker=Alice. Invariant tested: self-revocation followed by re-authorization via pre-signed message must not create a bypass window or loop. Scoped impact: gate bypass — Bob circumvents gate restrictions by cycling authorization state within a single transaction. Proof idea: unit test multicall sequence of revoke-then-reauthorize; assert gate check outcome is consistent.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified / EcrecoverRatifier.cancelRoot] Can an authorized operator Bob, authorized by Alice (the maker), call EcrecoverRatifier.cancelRoot(alice, root) — which checks `maker == msg.sender || IMidnight(MIDNIGHT).isAuthorized(maker, msg.sender)` — to cancel Alice's active offer root, effectively DoS-ing all of Alice's live offers in a gated market without Alice's consent, and is this an intended consequence of coarse-grained authorization? Preconditions: Alice has active offers under root R in a gated market; Bob is authorized by Alice on Midnight. Call sequence: (1) Bob calls EcrecoverRatifier.cancelRoot(alice, R); (2) isRootCanceled[alice][R] = true; (3) all takers attempting to fill Alice's offers get RootCanceled(). Invariant tested: offer cancellation must require explicit maker consent; coarse authorization must not allow operators to unilaterally cancel maker's offer roots. Scoped impact: fund freeze / offer bug — Alice's offers are permanently canceled without her consent, preventing her from earning yield in a gated market. Proof idea: unit test authorized operator calling cancelRoot; assert that cancellation requires maker's direct action or a separate scoped authorization.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: SetterRatifier.setIsRootRatified / Midnight.setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized operator Bob call SetterRatifier.setIsRootRatified(alice, maliciousRoot, true) — which checks `maker == msg.sender || IMidnight(MIDNIGHT).isAuthorized(maker, msg.sender)` — to ratify a malicious Merkle root on Alice's behalf, then construct offers under that root with arbitrary terms (any tick, any maxAssets, any group), effectively allowing Bob to fill offers as if Alice had approved them, bypassing any gate that restricts Alice's counterparties? Preconditions: Bob is authorized by Alice; market has IEnterGate; maliciousRoot contains an offer with attacker-favorable terms. Call sequence: (1) Bob calls SetterRatifier.setIsRootRatified(alice, maliciousRoot, true); (2) Bob constructs Offer{maker=alice, ratifier=setterRatifier, ...} matching a leaf in maliciousRoot; (3) Bob calls take() filling the offer. Invariant tested: ratifier root approval must require explicit maker consent; authorized operators must not be able to ratify arbitrary offer sets on behalf of the maker. Scoped impact: unauthorized offer fill — attacker fills offers under Alice's identity with attacker-controlled terms, draining Alice's funds or creating unwanted debt. Proof idea: unit test authorized operator calling setIsRootRatified with attacker-controlled root; assert offer fill succeeds and measure impact.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: Midnight.setIsAuthorized / EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker exploit the fact that `isAuthorized[authorizer][authorized]` is a flat boolean mapping (not scoped by market, action, or time) to obtain authorization via a signature intended for a low-risk action (e.g., withdrawing collateral in market A), then use that authorization to bypass an IEnterGate in a completely different market B by calling take() with taker=victim in market B, since the gate check `canIncreaseDebt(seller)` uses the victim's address and the authorization check `isAuthorized[taker][msg.sender]` is market-agnostic? Preconditions: victim authorized attacker for market A operations; market B has IEnterGate blocking victim; attacker is not blocked by gate. Call sequence: (1) victim authorizes attacker for market A; (2) attacker calls take() in market B with taker=victim; (3) gate checks canIncreaseDebt(victim) — victim is not blocked; (4) attacker increases victim's debt in market B. Invariant tested: authorization must be scoped or gate checks must account for the actual initiator; coarse authorization must not enable cross-market gate bypass. Scoped impact: gate bypass — attacker uses victim's authorization to enter a market on victim's behalf, creating unwanted debt positions. Proof idea: fuzz market parameters; assert that authorization granted for one market cannot be used to bypass gates in another market.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: Midnight.liquidate / ILiquidatorGate.canLiquidate] Can an attacker who is blocked by ILiquidatorGate (canLiquidate(attacker) returns false) bypass the liquidator gate by first getting authorized by a whitelisted liquidator W (isAuthorized[W][attacker]=true), then calling liquidate() as msg.sender=attacker with the gate checking canLiquidate(msg.sender)=canLiquidate(attacker)=false — confirming the gate correctly blocks the attacker — but then exploiting the fact that the attacker can call Midnight.setIsAuthorized on behalf of W to authorize a new address C that is not gated, and have C liquidate? Preconditions: attacker is blocked by liquidatorGate; W is a whitelisted liquidator who has authorized attacker; attacker wants to liquidate borrower B. Call sequence: (1) W authorizes attacker; (2) attacker calls Midnight.setIsAuthorized(C, true, W) where C is attacker-controlled; (3) C calls liquidate() — gate checks canLiquidate(C); (4) if gate doesn't know C, it may return true. Invariant tested: liquidator gate must not be bypassable by creating new authorized addresses that the gate has not evaluated. Scoped impact: gate bypass — unauthorized liquidator seizes collateral, potentially at unfavorable terms for the borrower. Proof idea: unit test with a whitelist-based liquidator gate; assert that authorization chain cannot introduce unlisted liquidators.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Does the `abi.encode(AUTHORIZATION_TYPEHASH, authorization)` encoding correctly handle the `Authorization` struct where `bool isAuthorized` is ABI-encoded as a 32-byte padded value, and can an attacker craft a signature where the bool field is encoded as a non-zero non-one value (e.g., 2) that `ecrecover` accepts but the Solidity ABI decoder interprets as `true`, creating a signature that authorizes when the signer intended to revoke? Preconditions: attacker has a signature where the isAuthorized byte is non-standard; ecrecover returns the correct signer address. Call sequence: (1) attacker crafts raw signature bytes with isAuthorized=2 in the ABI encoding; (2) submits to EcrecoverAuthorizer; (3) Solidity decodes bool as true; (4) authorization is granted. Invariant tested: EIP-712 struct encoding must be canonical; non-standard bool encoding must not produce valid signatures with unintended semantics. Scoped impact: unauthorized authorization — attacker grants authorization using a signature the signer did not intend as an authorization. Proof idea: fuzz the raw bytes of the Authorization encoding; assert that only canonical bool encodings (0 or 1) produce valid signatures.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker exploit Ethereum's ecrecover signature malleability (substituting (v, r, s) with (v^1, r, -s mod n) to produce a second valid signature for the same message) to submit a second valid authorization transaction after the first has already consumed the nonce, given that the nonce is incremented on the first submission — meaning the second malleable signature would fail due to nonce mismatch — but could there be a race condition where both signatures are submitted in the same block before state is committed? Preconditions: victim submits a valid authorization signature; attacker observes the signature in the mempool. Call sequence: (1) victim submits Authorization with (v, r, s) and nonce=N; (2) attacker submits malleable signature (v', r, s') for same message with nonce=N; (3) both are in the same block; (4) one succeeds, one fails. Invariant tested: signature malleability must not allow double-submission of the same authorization intent; nonce must prevent replay even of malleable variants. Scoped impact: nonce desync — if malleable variant is processed first, victim's original transaction fails; authorization state may differ from victim's intent. Proof idea: submit both canonical and malleable signatures in the same block; assert exactly one succeeds and nonce advances by exactly 1.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker who observes a victim's signed Authorization{isAuthorized=false} (a revocation message) for nonce N, submit it before the victim's intended Authorization{isAuthorized=true} for nonce N+1, causing the nonce to advance to N+1 via the revocation, and then submit the victim's Authorization{isAuthorized=true} for nonce N+1 themselves (since the message is public), granting authorization to an address the victim intended to revoke, in a scenario where the victim signed both messages but intended them to be submitted in order? Preconditions: victim signed revocation (nonce=N) and a new authorization (nonce=N+1) and distributed both; attacker observes both in the mempool. Call sequence: (1) attacker submits revocation (nonce=N) first; (2) attacker submits authorization (nonce=N+1); (3) net result: authorization is granted, not revoked. Invariant tested: sequential nonce submission must preserve the ordering intent of the signer; out-of-order submission must not invert the intended authorization state. Scoped impact: unauthorized authorization — attacker inverts victim's intended revocation by reordering signed messages. Proof idea: submit signed messages in reverse order; assert final authorization state matches the last message's intent regardless of submission order.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker exploit the nonce desync between EcrecoverAuthorizer (which tracks nonce[authorizer]) and Midnight.setIsAuthorized (which has no nonce) to create a state where Alice has used Midnight.setIsAuthorized directly to authorize/revoke Bob multiple times, but EcrecoverAuthorizer.nonce[Alice] is still at 0, allowing an attacker to submit any pre-signed Authorization message Alice ever created (even ones she considered superseded by direct Midnight calls) since the nonce check only validates against EcrecoverAuthorizer's own counter? Preconditions: Alice has used Midnight.setIsAuthorized directly multiple times; Alice also signed an Authorization message with nonce=0 long ago that she considers expired. Call sequence: (1) Alice calls Midnight.setIsAuthorized(bob, false, alice) directly; (2) attacker submits Alice's old signed Authorization{authorized=bob, isAuthorized=true, nonce=0}; (3) EcrecoverAuthorizer.nonce[alice]=0, so it passes; (4) Bob is re-authorized. Invariant tested: authorization state managed via direct Midnight calls must be reflected in or protected from EcrecoverAuthorizer nonce-based replay. Scoped impact: unauthorized authorization — attacker replays an old signed message to restore a revoked authorization, bypassing gate restrictions. Proof idea: unit test direct Midnight authorization followed by EcrecoverAuthorizer replay; assert old signed messages cannot restore revoked authorizations.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified] Can an attacker exploit the fact that EcrecoverRatifier uses `block.chainid` in its domain separator (not `INITIAL_CHAIN_ID`) while Midnight uses `INITIAL_CHAIN_ID` for market IDs, so that after a hard fork where chainId changes from C to C', a maker's offer signature (signed for chainId=C) becomes invalid on the new chain (C') but the market still exists with the same ID — and can the attacker use this to DoS the maker's offers on the new chain by ensuring the maker cannot fill offers until they re-sign with the new chainId? Preconditions: hard fork changes chainId; maker has active offers signed with old chainId; market exists on new chain with same ID. Call sequence: (1) hard fork occurs; (2) taker attempts to fill maker's offer using old signature; (3) EcrecoverRatifier recomputes domain separator with new chainId; (4) ecrecover returns wrong address; (5) Unauthorized() revert. Invariant tested: offer validity must be consistent with market validity across chain forks; chainId inconsistency between market ID and ratifier domain must not create permanent offer invalidation. Scoped impact: fund freeze — maker's offers are permanently invalid on the new chain until re-signed; lenders cannot exit positions. Proof idea: simulate hard fork by changing block.chainid; assert offer fill fails and document the re-signing requirement.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: Midnight.take / IEnterGate.canIncreaseCredit / IEnterGate.canIncreaseDebt] Can an attacker bypass an IEnterGate that checks `canIncreaseDebt(seller)` by constructing a take where the seller's debt does not actually increase (sellerDebtIncrease == 0 because sellerCreditDecrease == units, i.e., the seller has enough existing credit to cover the units), so the gate check `offer.market.enterGate == address(0) || sellerDebtIncrease == 0 || IEnterGate(offer.market.enterGate).canIncreaseDebt(seller)` short-circuits on `sellerDebtIncrease == 0` without calling the gate, allowing a gated seller to reduce their credit position without gate approval? Preconditions: seller has existing credit >= units; market has IEnterGate; gate would return false for canIncreaseDebt(seller). Call sequence: (1) seller has credit=100; (2) taker takes 50 units from seller's sell offer; (3) sellerCreditDecrease=50, sellerDebtIncrease=0; (4) gate check skipped; (5) seller's credit reduced. Invariant tested: gate must be called for all entry-relevant state changes; credit reduction without gate approval must be evaluated for gate intent. Scoped impact: gate bypass — seller exits credit position without gate approval, potentially violating the gate's access control intent. Proof idea: unit test with gate that blocks all seller interactions; assert gate is not called when sellerDebtIncrease==0 and document whether this is intended.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: Midnight.take / IEnterGate.canIncreaseCredit] Can an attacker bypass an IEnterGate that checks `canIncreaseCredit(buyer)` by constructing a take where the buyer's credit does not increase (buyerCreditIncrease == 0 because the buyer has existing debt >= units, so buying units reduces debt rather than increasing credit), causing the gate check `buyerCreditIncrease == 0 || IEnterGate(enterGate).canIncreaseCredit(buyer)` to short-circuit, allowing a gated buyer to reduce their debt without gate approval? Preconditions: buyer has existing debt >= units; market has IEnterGate; gate would return false for canIncreaseCredit(buyer). Call sequence: (1) buyer has debt=100; (2) buyer takes 50 units from a buy offer; (3) buyerCreditIncrease=0 (debt reduced by 50); (4) gate check skipped; (5) buyer's debt reduced. Invariant tested: gate must be called for all entry-relevant state changes including debt reduction; the gate's intent to restrict the buyer must not be bypassable via debt-reduction path. Scoped impact: gate bypass — gated buyer reduces debt (exits position) without gate approval. Proof idea: unit test with gate blocking all buyer interactions; assert gate is not called when buyerCreditIncrease==0.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker who has obtained a victim's signed Authorization{isAuthorized=true, deadline=T} replay it on a different deployed instance of EcrecoverAuthorizer (e.g., a second deployment at a different address) that points to the same Midnight contract, given that the domain separator includes `address(this)` (the EcrecoverAuthorizer address) — meaning replay across different EcrecoverAuthorizer instances is prevented by the domain separator — but can the attacker deploy a new EcrecoverAuthorizer at a predictable CREATE2 address and trick the victim into signing for that address, then use it to authorize themselves on the shared Midnight? Preconditions: attacker deploys EcrecoverAuthorizer2 at address A2 pointing to same Midnight; victim signs Authorization for A2 thinking it's a different contract. Call sequence: (1) attacker deploys EcrecoverAuthorizer2; (2) victim signs Authorization{deadline=T, nonce=0} for A2; (3) attacker submits to EcrecoverAuthorizer2; (4) Midnight.setIsAuthorized is called with attacker as authorized. Invariant tested: EcrecoverAuthorizer deployments must be distinguishable; victims must not be tricked into signing for attacker-controlled authorizer instances. Scoped impact: unauthorized authorization — attacker gains authorization on Midnight via a fake authorizer contract. Proof idea: deploy two EcrecoverAuthorizer instances; assert signatures are not cross-replayable and document the social engineering risk.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker exploit the fact that `keccak256(abi.encode(AUTHORIZATION_TYPEHASH, authorization))` encodes the Authorization struct by value (not by reference), and that `abi.encode` of a struct with a `bool` field pads it to 32 bytes, to verify whether the AUTHORIZATION_TYPEHASH constant `0x81d0284fb0e2cde18d0553b06189d6f7613c96a01bb5b5e7828eade6a0dcac91` correctly corresponds to `keccak256('Authorization(address authorizer,address authorized,bool isAuthorized,uint256 nonce,uint256 deadline)')`, and if there is a mismatch, whether signatures produced by standard EIP-712 wallets would fail or succeed unexpectedly? Preconditions: standard EIP-712 wallet computes typehash from the type string; contract uses hardcoded constant. Call sequence: (1) wallet computes typehash from type string; (2) wallet signs Authorization; (3) contract uses hardcoded AUTHORIZATION_TYPEHASH; (4) if mismatch, ecrecover returns wrong address. Invariant tested: hardcoded typehash constants must exactly match the keccak256 of their type strings; any mismatch breaks all signature verification. Scoped impact: fund freeze — all EcrecoverAuthorizer signatures fail if typehash is wrong; or unauthorized authorization if typehash collision exists. Proof idea: compute keccak256('Authorization(address authorizer,address authorized,bool isAuthorized,uint256 nonce,uint256 deadline)') in a test and assert it equals AUTHORIZATION_TYPEHASH.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IGate.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified] Can an attacker verify whether the OFFER_TYPEHASH constant `0x980a4cfc9766df84667f316d76e10cefc8caf04fb4cd4a9fca00a8e7b34f619c` in HashLib correctly corresponds to `keccak256(bytes.concat(OFFER_TYPE, COLLATERAL_PARAMS_TYPE, MARKET_TYPE))` where OFFER_TYPE includes all fields of the Offer struct in the correct order and encoding, and if the `callbackData bytes` field is hashed as `keccak256(offer.callbackData)` in hashOffer but the typehash string uses `bytes callbackData` (dynamic type), whether the EIP-712 encoding is correct and consistent with what hardware wallets would produce? Preconditions: maker uses a hardware wallet to sign offer trees; wallet computes typehash from type string. Call sequence: (1) wallet computes OFFER_TYPEHASH from type string; (2) wallet signs offer tree root; (3) EcrecoverRatifier uses hardcoded OFFER_TYPEHASH; (4) if mismatch, all offer signatures fail. Invariant tested: EIP-712 typehash for complex nested structs with dynamic types must be computed correctly; bytes fields must be encoded as keccak256(value) in the struct hash.
